story:
It was a sunny Saturday and Cayden was upstairs, tinkering with his phone and the programs he’d been building, when his mum yelled, “Get in the car. We’re buying shoes and books. Ask Jaz and Cuz to come. Maybe a movie if you behave.”

He texted his best mates from down the street. They jogged over while he washed his face and half-fixed his hair, and the three of them piled into the car. Laddie, the tiny terrier, curled under the front seat where Cayden had hidden him. His mum kept double-checking the iron and the oven, then got caught on her phone again. The boys baked in the driveway heat, windows up, sun blazing.

“Kmart. Seriously?” Jaz groaned.

“Yeah,” said Cayden, poking at new icons he’d added to his phone. That’s when it happened: a blinding flash, a sonic thump like a jet, and a puff of smoke. Laddie whimpered. Then everything went still. The driveway, the house, the shops—gone. Outside, the world blazed green. Giant trees. Tangled vines. Air like a hot, wet towel.

“What did you do?” Cuz asked, not joking.

“I was… trying to make a time machine,” Cayden admitted.

“It worked,” Jaz said, pointing. A massive, cassowary-bright bird—taller than an emu—strode through the undergrowth. Cayden and Jaz cracked their doors for a better look. Laddie shot out, all bark and swagger, chasing the giant bird as if he were ten feet tall.

“Oh no! Mum will flatten me. Laddie! Back!” Cayden yelled, and the three boys scrambled after the dog. The forest pressed in—steamy, buzzing, full of shrieks from strange birds roosting overhead. The bird pounded along. Laddie kept after it, fearless and loud, until it skidded to a halt.

“Why’d it stop?” Cuz asked, but Jaz was already pointing. A wombat the size of a truck lumbered across their path. Farther on, a hulking kangaroo shape angled through the trees. Prehistoric Australia wrapped around them.

“Dog biscuits,” Cayden whispered. “If we get close enough, we can grab him.”

They crept forward, dodging vines and a black beetle as big as Cayden’s hand, trying not to touch anything that could sting. The forest opened to a waterfall pouring into a bright pool, sunlight scattering into sparkles. Laddie was there, tongue in the water, panting hard. He saw them, tail going like a propeller, and bounded over. Cayden scooped him up, biscuits forgotten, face covered in damp, joyful licks.

The giant bird had drifted away. The only mammals nearby were three breathless boys and one very pleased terrier. “Back to the car,” Cayden said. “Follow our tracks.”

They picked their way through the undergrowth, stepping around steaming piles of megafauna manure and keeping clear of oversized insects. At the car, Cayden took a breath and tapped the icon again. The flash, the thump—and the driveway returned. Laddie curled up on the seat and was asleep in seconds. The boys whooped. Even Kmart sounded okay now.

“Good one,” Cuz said, grinning. “You actually did it.”

Mum walked over. “Shopping's off. I've got to see if Grandma's all right. Go and make yourselves some lunch. Off you go!” 

The boys and Laddie got off, stood, and stared as she drove off down the street. 

“I think we made it to prehistoric Australia,” Cayden said. “And we made it back.”

They nodded, still grinning, and headed inside to raid the fridge. Cayden knew exactly where his mum hid the chocolate. Time travel, megafauna, and a waterfall—no wonder he was starving.
